Hey, this is Dave D. Welcome to the Ultimate No BS Referral Machine where you're going to discover how to generate a constant flow of new business coming from your own customers, clients, or patients. I have with me marketing legend Dan Kennedy. Dan, here we are again. Here we are again. Here we are again with our nice cups. Can you turn that out so the camera can... Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. Okay, fantastic. So, we're going to be talking about referrals for the next four hours. That's all the brain building you're going to see. That's exactly it. And how you can generate referrals. I want to start off with a fundamental question right away though is, what are the benefits of having referrals other than the obvious one that you're bringing in more business? Well, so probably equally obvious. Most people understand that by and large, the referred customer-client patient is a quote-quote better customer-client or patient than one that is gotten cold off the street. It's obviously not always true, but if you were going to play the odds, they have more knowns coming in, therefore they have less sales resistance. So it depends on your definition of better, but it starts there for people who are not particularly good at selling, who are uncomfortable at selling, who have a staff that's not particularly good at selling. The referred customer-client patient is definitely better than one that comes in cold because of the amount of pre-selling that occurred. Let me ask you this really quickly though, because I think there's a misconception that you don't have to sell at all when a referral comes in. Well, a prospect is a prospect. A first-time buyer is a first-time buyer, yet unconverted to a true customer or client, and a customer or client is not yet converted to a champion. It doesn't really matter where they come from. You may be, again, you may need less skill in many situations in order to get the same result if you're dealing with somebody who came by referral. You may be able to take some shortcuts in your selling process, but it is, to your point, it's very dangerous to make assumptions that you get to have unearned success. The second reason that they're so desirable is because of price and price elasticity. The referred customer or client or patient is less sales resistant, therefore they are automatically less price or fee resistant. You buy yourself a little room to sell at premium prices to present at least premium priced options and have success with them, with the referred person versus the person who comes in cold. For a variety of reasons, the trust issue, the fact that they may shop less before they get to you, so the cold prospect, if we generate a cold prospect online, that person probably does not just see us. They look at four or five alternatives of us before they get to us. If you ask me, gee, I got this whatever problem I got, I got gophers in my backyard. Do you know anybody that can make gophers go away? I say, yeah, my garden guy is the greatest garden guy. You may not go look at any other garden guy's sites before my guy comes there, and if he doesn't sell you the $15,000 year long no-go for a program, immediately you may still not go shop. So it gives us a certain amount of price elasticity. And the reason that's timing critical now is because in many product and service categories, the pool of available customers is actually shrinking. So as are, at least domestically, as are US middle classes shrinking, the percentage of people with discretionary spending for broad categories shrinks as well. So your low half of the middle class has basically gone over a five-year period from spending 50% of their income on core necessities, house payment, utility, car, insurance, maybe 10 categories, and having 50% left over to either save investors' spend and most spend, to now spending 70% of their income on core necessities with only 30% left over. So as the pool of available customers and their available spend tightens, it is ever more important to maximize referrals to get the best possible customer you can in the door with the least price resistance. Last, there's sort of a hidden benefit to the referred customer. It means that your existing customer is referring, which makes it all the more difficult for him to leave you. So if I have convinced four buddies to buy season tickets to the Cleveland Browns, which That would be a sales job. That would be a sales job. So it's unlikely. It's a very poor example. But you know, well, right now there's insane hysteria about Manzell. So let's say I get four buddies to buy season tickets. Very difficult for me next year not to renew my season tickets. If it's just me, I get to make that decision in a vacuum. So same thing happens with anything. If I get you to go to my dentist and I get my neighbor to go to my dentist, I got to keep going on my dentist. So so there's this hidden kind of closed loop to this. It's a psychology. Chaldeany talks about that in influence. So so that that's another reason to focus on it to concentrate on it. So I think most business owners intuitively know that they should be doing this. You just gave some reasons that they probably didn't have them thought of. Why don't they do it? Why don't they have a system in place? And as an add on to that question, a lot of people will say, oh, I get my business through referral when they really don't. They really don't have a system or any way to track it. No, to your second point, most business owners don't know where their business comes from. By the way, right? You know, they are they are they are running on, you know, the Obama hope strategy enunciated in the first campaign, which as we see is really not much of a strategy at all. So they just sort of are coming to work in the morning and hope that somebody shows up and somebody says, and when somebody says we do, we get a lot of referrals or we do, you know, we're we're really good at that. If you force them to examine their statistics, so total number of customers active this month, this quarter, this year against total number of referrals who came in this month, this quarter, this year, you know, it's in the single digit percentages of productivity. I mean, it's pathetic. Yeah, they're just happy about it. So they remember it. Right. So the reason that they don't. So first is they actually think we're doing great. It's all we could get. Right. Secondly, there's a profound sense of entitlement in professionals, business owners, sale people, etc. That so their their thinking loop is I do a really good job at my core, you know, deliverable. So you come here, you get great dental care, you come here, you get a great meal, you come here, you get your haircut exactly the way it should be cut every single time. So you get your two pay wash, whatever it is. So I was going to say something about your hair. Okay, I'm going to let it go. I'll make them. So so I do a really good job with your core deliverable. On top of that, I provide really good service. Therefore, I am entitled to referrals. Okay. Therefore, I will get referrals because I get what I'm entitled to in a just universe. Right. So they put in the third thing, which is I'm a really good guy, or they put in the fourth thing, which is I do really a job. I give really good service. I'm a really good guy, and I got five kids to feed. I have a need. Right. Therefore, I will get, you know, referrals. Whereas everything I just described is like sort of a minimum ante to get to the table. Right. It's not, you know, so there's that. There's this entitlement idea. Sometimes there's ego in the way that the act of asking for, of doing anything overtly to stimulate referrals is a shadow on my ego. So I won't do it. There's that reason. Some people, it's their staff is running their business. And when they go to try and do anything proactive to stimulate referrals are really pretty much proactive to do anything else. The staff now trots out their list, you know, which the top of the list is always blackmail, which is well, okay, we can do that. But then we're going to have to not do these three things we're already doing now, like turn on the lights and, you know, so, well, okay, I give right. So there's that. So that points even bigger problems. Oh, yeah. Obviously. It's symptomatic. Right. But a lot of business owners are in perpetual hostage mode to their staff. In our world, there's the business owner goes to the seminar. Business owner comes back from seminar all jazzed up and he's got five pages of ideas. He's got, you know, some CDs under his arm and I'm ready to go. And the staff has their little meeting. You know, we've seen this before. Right. Pretty much if we just go into slow mo and wait it out, it'll all wear off and things go back to normal in about two weeks. And if it doesn't, then here's our, here's our scheme. Here's our plan of how we handle this, which one is extortion. Right. It's well, okay, doc. We can start doing these concern calls at the end of every day. But that means we won't be able to bill insurance on time. Oh, you know, which is one step away from, you know, okay, we'll do that, but we'll have to cut your baby's head off and eat it. And so, which, so the staff gets it away. There's outright laziness and ultimate because it's a lot easier just to throw money at advertising. Sure. It's a lot easier to do that than it is to think this all the way through and put a referral system in place. So there's a lot of sloth. And last, there's the only legitimate, you know, not really knowing what to do and not knowing how to do it. Do you think there's some fear involved though too? I mean, some folks are just like, Now, I think that's the ego stuff. You think that's the same thing? You always really think it's ego. Yeah. Okay. I think it's about how I'll be perceived and I'll be perceived negatively. Or needy. Or, well, yeah. So unsuccessful, in need, pushy, salesy. You know, there's a whole range of negative emotions, but they're all, you know, ego driven. And, and ironically, if you actually do a good job, you do anything significant in people's lives, you deliver goods, then it's also actually an act of anti empathy. Absolutely. I mean, it's not, you know, it's not really being helpful. Right. So you're really letting your ego get in the way, not just of your money, but actually of your service to community, you know, et cetera. But yeah, I think people are very much inhibited by what they think people will think of what it is that they do. Now, going back to the first point they made, you often, I've heard you say in the past about marketing that people think, well, because I'm the best at what I do. Right. I should just get customers, clients, or patients coming in. It's the entitlement. It's the same thing with referrals. Yeah. It's the exact same thing. Yeah. So the entitlement list is because I've got a PhD and this, and my competitor doesn't, because I've got a cooler office, because I do better work, because I've been here longer, because I, you know, there's a long list of reasons for entitlement. Business owners, in reality, often are not much different than the union mentality of a worker, which is the longer I'm at this, the easier it should get to get customers, and the more money I should get. Right. Right. You know, I've been in this community for 37 years, and he opened his office three days ago. And, you know, I'm entitled to, well, you know, and people don't say, they don't all the way enunciate it as entitlement. Sure. But that's exactly what it is. Sure. Well, they're not even consciously aware of it, probably. Probably. So, so now we know why business owners don't ask for referrals. I guess the question is to the, the entrepreneur and the business owner watching this right now is, well, how come customers just don't automatically refer if I do a good job, if I'm a nice guy, if I have five kids, so on and so forth. So there's two big broad answers. Okay. Well, almost nothing happens automatically except bad things. Almost nothing happens automatically except bad things. So a car's life extended to 100,000 miles. Or I just saw, and I don't think they've made a commercial out of it like Subway did with Jared the morons, but some woman was on the news. Her Toyota just turned its millionth mile. Millionth. Yeah. She's got some kind of route. 800 miles a week or something. Million miles on this, on this Toyota pick. Well, that didn't happen automatically. She got, they're all changed. She got, she took care of this truck. The trucks collapse at planned obsolescence of 25,000 miles right after the warranty and everything starts going bad. That happens automatically. You don't have to do a thing. Just drive it. Okay. So it happens automatically. Good things never do. The second big reason is it's not their job. The customer is not your salesperson. Right. Their job is to give you money. Right. They're fulfilling that. It's not their job. They got other stuff to do. They got, you know, so those are two big reasons. The reason that a lot, that they don't refer automatically that a lot of business owners don't understand is that it's a much higher threshold of risk and pain to refer that it is to continue to patronize. People will even continue to patronize businesses that don't do a very good job because devil, devil I know is better than the devil I don't. It's inconvenient. It's habit. It's, you know, I stopped at a coffee, just sit at a counter reading newspaper. I have some coffee. I might want to hear this morning. There's three people at the counter next to me. They're all talking about how bad this restaurant is. They're all eating. It's clear they go there regularly. Right. And it's all I can do not to say, you know, but, but, but see, but what's their conversation about the place? It's all bad. Right. So the restaurants are great example because we have all had this experience and that is, there is a restaurant you go to regularly. It's probably a neighborhood place, not a chain. You, you, it's maybe not obscure. It's not, you know, and you and your wife love it. And so you tell a friend about it and they go there and have imagined or actual, it matters not. Right. They have a really bad experience. They can move away. You can go seven years without seeing them, talking to them, having any contact with them, except a Christmas card going back and forth once a year. You finally see them somewhere. The first thing out of their mouth will be to tell you about that restaurant that you told them to go to. That you told them to go to. There was seven years ago. So everybody's had that. Right. Absolutely. We got it. Right. So that reminds us when we're about to make this mistake again. Right. Right. So the threshold for patronage and continued patronage is much lower than the threshold to refer. And then there's different, so there's referrals that only happen by request. So you move into the area and you say, where do you take your cleaning? So I'll answer that question. But I'm not running around telling people about my dry clothes. Right. You're not at an evangelist's. That threshold's even higher. Right. Right. So the list of why this doesn't happen generally is number one, some customers have no source of influence. They actually don't have the capability to refer. Okay. The few friends they have think of them as an idiot. Okay. If they do refer a place, everybody knows. Not to go there. Not to go there. Okay. So that exists. Right. But that's a very small percentage of people in general and of people you would have as a customer. Second, no motivation. Right. They're just, they're not demotivated enough to leave, but they are not motivated. In any way, shape or form to refer. Right. And you think their continued patronage is a sign of enthusiastic zeal for which you are entitled extra reward. Right. So you project onto that something that really isn't there at all. Right. So no motivation, no system, no mechanism, no process for doing it. So even if I am inclined, I really don't have a way, an effective way to tell you about my Dennis Dry Cleaner CPA, whatever. I'm not going to create it. That you can act on. Right. Right. And then last, no express gratitude. So the person who does actually overcome all those obstacles and actively bring you somebody, send you somebody and is then not recognized and rewarded. So most nothing, perhaps a casual, next time I see you, oh, by the way, Dave, thanks for telling so and so about me. An email, right? A stock off the shelf, clearly off the shelf, grudging obligatory, you know, send out car thing. Right. So that says to me, well, he really doesn't value this. Right? Why should I go to great, you know, effort? Sure. You know, there's a story about this guy out fishing and he's in a boat fishing all day long and he didn't caught anything because he's no good at it. And he's about to give up when all of a sudden his frog jumps up out of the water and it throws a trout right in his boat. This is the greatest thing I've ever seen. This is a frog sitting on the edge of the boat now. This is the greatest thing. He looks around and says, no, I don't have anything to worry about. I don't have a bait. I don't have a way to reward you, Mr. Frog. All I got left is this flask of bourbon. I've been dragging out of it. Have some bourbon. And the frog glugs down some bourbon, jumps back in the water. He's back in about three minutes with three trouts. So you know, when you recognize and when you reward, you get more of the behavior that you recognized and reward. When you don't recognize a reward generally, you don't get a repeated behavior. I've had that and where I've referred a bunch of people and haven't been, and then I actually stopped patronizing. Well, it can go that bad. It can go that bad, right? It can go that bad. You can actually resent it at some point. I can't think of one that I've actually reacted that way. I'm capable of reacting that way. Really? I think everyone would be shocked. I just can't actually think of one. But you're absolutely right in that the person who does something above and beyond and extra for a business owner who they give money to in exchange for goods and services actually has an expectation of some sort of expressed gratitude. And they are disappointed when they don't get it. In general, you know, Edward Kramer who invented the thank you gram, Kramer's line was the most pitiful, pathetic, useless person on earth gets up every morning thinking that he deserves a thank you note today and a congratulations telegram from somebody for something he did. And he's bitterly disappointed at the end of the day that, you know, that that didn't happen, right? So if you really did do something useful and beneficial for somebody, you expect and you expect it promptly. You know, I have one of the charities I do some pro bono work. And they have 56 excuses for why they do it. But so they send out thank you notes to new donors once a month. And I keep screaming, no, no, no, no, no, no, no. Person gave you a donation on Monday. They're looking for their thank you on Tuesday, right? And they are bitterly disappointed by Thursday. And if it arrives three weeks later, it either has no impact at all or it has the impact as a negative impact like it's like, well, screw you. Right? Right. Yeah, I know you just did this because yeah, I get it. You let them stack up and it's a form letter. Right. Right. So, yeah. So just right now we're laying the foundations, but in the future sessions that we're going to do coming up, we're going to go through detail about everything. We are. Yeah, we are. We are going to, we've got a lot of stuff to show. But let's talk about the concept of a referral machine and how do you put a referral, what exactly is a referral machine? The opposite of what everybody has. Right. So if you don't know what to do. So the first of all, it is intentional, right? Which means that there's a process that happens the same way every time like clockwork that is triggered by the referral or the absence of referrals within a certain period of time. So it is very intentional. It is invested in. Nobody gets a free machine. So it takes some work to put it in place. I think in England they give poor people air conditioners. I do. Do they give air conditioners? So we've got UK viewers. Let us know. So I'm not sure. Are there free air conditioners? Or they give them air conditioning chips or something. But air conditioning what? Chips. That you can, you know, like that you can go to the store and use to get an air conditioner. Oh, okay. And you can pay the pool and bill or something. Okay. All right. So, so anyway, there's investment. And most people invest almost without thought in external marketing and are grudging and grumpy about investing anything in internal marketing. So every time this comes up, again, mostly because of the entitlement thinking, everybody thinks they should get their referrals for free. Everybody understands they got to pay money to go get a new customer off the street, but they think that they ought to get the referrals for it. Right. Well, it doesn't work that way. If you actually are going to build a machine, you're going to make an investment in building it, and you're going to make an investment in running it every single day, every single week, every single month. And then third is integration, meaning that, see, three eyes, it's a literature. I love how you did that. You spent hours coming up with that. It's all these years. So integration, right? So there's two, it's integrated into your business process. So there's a certain point in your new customer intake when there's a referral discussion, there's a certain point where referral tools are dispensed, so forth. It's integrated into your culture, both customer and staff. So everybody gets it. We really are a referral-driven practice, and I get that. I see it, I hear it, I smell it. So there's integration. So what are the goals of a referral machine? And by the way, if you have questions, we'll be taking some questions as well. So if you've got questions for Dan about referrals, make sure you ask those. So Dan, what are the goals of a referral machine? Well, first is that it brings it to top-up consciousness of your customers, clients, or patients, meaning that literally every day of their lives you are present. I didn't because I'm not motivated enough about any of the ones I patronize to do that. And even if I was, I don't have a good method, et cetera, et cetera. But if I did, I could have. Automatically. I start advocating Disney. Take it my kids are universal. How do you know how to do that? Let me tell you why you want to go to Disney. So, but Disney's on the top of my consciousness all the time. Second thing is ease of doing. So there has to be, your referral machine has to be built around ways and provide ways that it's very easy for me to refer people to you. Because he wanted recognition, acknowledgement. He's looking for status in my world, which we then give him. So status by doing it. The last form of status is that you get status in the eyes of the person you are referring, that you are doing a beneficial favor when I tell you about somebody. The reaction I want from you is not a negative, oh crap, he's pitching me on his, say I want, oh thank you very much. So there's that. Reward and recognition again and measurement. So if you really want to be effective at this, you have to measure your effectiveness. Just like you would do with any work. You have to measure your productivity. You have to measure the productivity of each customer. You have to be paying attention to who does it and who doesn't do it and then do something about it. So you mentioned reward a number of times. Once I'm in an industry where my law, I can't reward referrals. How do you handle that? So you're leaping by the way. So nine out of ten times, that's false. So I hear it all the time. And for the most part it is people expanding the law to industry norms, to pure acceptable behavior and using it as an excuse. So that's the first thing. The narrow definition rarely precludes any and all type of reward. But if it does, you are now narrowed to creating obligation and dispensing recognition. Now those two things are still very, very powerful. But there's a lot of, again, what will people think? This is years ago, my chiropractor that I went to in Phoenix had, in all the treatments, in the rooms, we had quarterly promotion, referral promotion every quarter, but now different prices. Yes. So stuck up on the walls were the five things you could get this quarter for referral. It was a couple of them relevant. So the pillow thing is stuck up there. The three bottles in the lot, there's a picture of them stuck up there. But there's six movie tickets stuck up there. Arizona baseball cap stuck up there. So I remember she had a meeting one time, she hosted a meeting for the Arizona Cardiobrack Association. And most of these guys were struggling, her practice boomed, by the way. So they came to her office. And most were appalled and one was vocally and loudly appalled. Of course. And I remember that was at how unprofessional, unseemly, you know, I mean it's the guys that, well, in my treatment rooms, I have, you know, reproductions of Monet paintings, gorgeous frames. Right. And, you know, the answer to that guy is, yeah, but I have 36 referrals last month. So there's a lot of that, that really is not law. So I hear it a lot and it's very rarely true. You know, just simple things too, like one of the things is, and you don't do it, I don't think maybe you do directly for a referral, but anyone can do is the simple thank you note. You say you answer all of your white mail. And I think that helps start referrals too. Absolutely. So tell them, first of all, explain what white mail is and then why, then what you do. So, because anyone, it doesn't matter when you can do this, you can use this. Oh yeah. So part of a referral machine is its foundation that it sits on. So if you get a generator for your house, which I recommend, if you get a generator for your house, but you don't put a concrete pad down for it and you just stick it out there on the hill, it will not function very, very well. Right. So the foundation underneath the referral machine is as at least as important as the machine. So the minimum parts of the foundation are you got to do good work, all that, right? And then there's a relationship layer on top of that. And there's sort of semi-mechanized parts of that, customer newsletter. So there's the responsiveness to people who communicate with you or do things for you. And so it feeds upon itself, by the way. So white mail, leaping to that. White mail is a direct marketing term for anything that comes in the mail that doesn't have a customer service issue in it or in order. Right. So these are complaints. These are questions. These are success reports. These are thank yous. These are, I wanted to let you know, I told Billy Bob about you. You should be hearing from Billy Bob. This is what this stuff is. So depending upon the category of business you're in, you'll get more or less of it. It'll be more personal or less personal. The nature of what I do, a lot of it is lengthy and a lot of it is somewhat personal. But some of it is very, you know, run of the mill stuff. I have always and continue to do so answered all the white mail. That could be as simple as a out of boy, Dan, faxes back to bell. Or it could be a two page letter answering something Bill has raised. Bill, hey, I wanted to tell you I told so and so about you and maybe call. Now I'm going to recognize in that whether that guy ever calls me or not. Right. Because he went above and beyond. Sure. And even if he's ineffective at it, if he does it eight and 12 times, one of them's going to come my way. If he only does it once and never does it again, well, the odds aren't in my favor. So I got to reward each step of compliant, you know, behavior. And we don't, we just don't thank people enough for the things they do for us over above and beyond giving us money in exchange for goods and services. So so far we've learned about how to the importance of referrals, the hidden benefits of referrals, why the business owner, the entrepreneur doesn't ask for referrals. We talked about why the customer just doesn't refer and then even what to do if you're in a business that you can't reward referrals. This is nobody can say we're not doing nobody can say we're not training 100% training here. 100% training. You like that? Yeah. Okay. So I'm a pro. I'm a pro. I've got a great team. We're not. I think I see right here Q and A. Oh, we're going to get to the tea. So, so, hey, you guys just stay tuned for the teas because this is going to be something special. Okay. So what we're going to do is we're going to take some questions. You've got some questions for Dan about referrals. He now is the time to ask. All right. Whoa. Okay. So the first question is what are specific rewards that a company should give customers for referring other customers? The best. The best that the value of the customer will permit.
